artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Finally sitting down with my laptop now that both kids are in bed and Mark is grading labs at the dining room table. I wanted to catch you up after this very long stretch.

I’m still on the same seizure meds Dr. Patel at Blue Valley Neurology warned me about back in March 2024: levetiracetam 750 mg twice a day, valproic acid 250 mg three times a day, and clobazam 10 mg in the morning and 20 mg at night. He really thinks the Depakote is feeding into the depressive episode I had and those suicidal thoughts I finally admitted to Mark, which is what pushed me into those two weeks of FMLA from Children’s Mercy. I hate even writing that, but you keep telling me to be honest.

The meds are also killing our budget. Since December 2024, Mark’s insurance through Blue Valley Southwest has denied the Onfi refill twice, so I’ve been stretching doses. That $186 we had to pay out of pocket at the CVS on 151st Street is exactly why I swallowed my pride and asked you to pull from your retirement account. I still feel awful about that.

All of this is layered on top of Daniel’s stuff—his drop seizure on October 3, 2024 outside Mr. Jenkins’ science room at Blue Valley Middle (no helmet, of course) meant the nurse called me out of a treatment session at the clinic so I could sign the incident report and talk with the assistant principal about maybe moving him back to full‑time SPED. I keep thinking if my own seizures and depression were better controlled, I’d have more bandwidth to fight for him at school instead of just reacting to crises.

We are okay, just tired. I’m still doing sertraline 50 mg and weekly telehealth with Dr. Phillips, which helps. Thank you again for the money and for not making me feel like a failure.

Love,
Emily

metadata.subject: Catching you up on meds, money, and Daniel

artifacts.content:
Hi Karen,

I wanted to send a bit more context before our telehealth on Thursday.

The panic attack I told you about driving home in March 2024 was the turning point. I’d been up three nights in a row watching Daniel’s nighttime tonic clusters, getting maybe 2–3 hours of broken sleep. On the way back from Children’s Mercy that last night, I actually nodded off at the red light at 135th and Metcalf. An Overland Park police officer knocked on my window and gave me a warning, said I was lucky I hadn’t caused an accident. That’s when my PCP at Shawnee Mission Family Medicine started the sertraline 50 mg and sent me your way.

Since then, the sleep deprivation hasn’t really let up. On August 27, 2024, I watched on the baby monitor from our bedroom as Daniel had a cluster of six tonic seizures between 1:00 and 3:00 a.m. I ended up sleeping on the floor in his and Sophie’s room again so she wouldn’t wake up screaming like she did during his July cluster. Mark had early‑morning duty at Blue Valley Southwest, so it was just me.

I’m pinning a lot of hope on EPIDIOLEX, partly because if it works and we can taper his valproic acid and clobazam over the next 6–12 months, maybe we won’t be living in this permanent crisis mode. I’m scared, though, that if his behavior gets worse during titration, I won’t be emotionally steady enough to manage him, my own seizures, and my part‑time caseload at Children’s Mercy Pediatric Therapy Clinic.

On top of that, I still carry shame about my own status episode at work back in October 2023 in front of my 3rd‑grade therapy group, and the HR review and temporary reduction in hours that followed. It all feels connected—sleep, meds, work, parenting. I’d like to talk about how to set realistic limits before I burn out completely.

See you Thursday,
Emily

Seizure/meds/finance brain dump – for next neuro + PCP visits

1. My meds
- Currently: levetiracetam 750 mg BID, clobazam 10 mg AM/20 mg HS, valproic acid 250 mg TID (all started after status episode in October 2023 at Children’s Mercy with my 3rd‑grade OT group; Dr. Melissa Hargrove at KU Med epilepsy clinic).
- Dr. Patel at Blue Valley Neurology (March 2024) thinks valproate is worsening my depression and that two‑week FMLA stretch after I told Mark about suicidal thoughts.
- Since Dec 2024, stretching clobazam because Mark’s Blue Valley Southwest plan denied refills twice; paid $186 at CVS on 151st after borrowing from Mom’s retirement.

2. Daniel’s recent events
- 10/3/24: drop seizure outside Mr. Jenkins’ science room at Blue Valley Middle ~12:30 p.m., no helmet. Nurse pulled me out of treatment session at Children’s Mercy – discussed possible move back to full‑time SPED.
- 8/27/24: 6 tonic seizures between 1–3 a.m., watched on baby monitor from our bedroom at Willow Bend. Slept on kids’ floor so Sophie wouldn’t relive July cluster.
- 11/9/24: generalized tonic‑clonic at 10:30 a.m. children’s service at Colonial Presbyterian; stiffened, fell into aisle, lost bladder control. Usher cleared row, on‑call nurse helped. Several clinic parents saw.

3. Money/insurance
- Nov 2023: $1,847 Children’s Mercy long‑term EEG bill → collections.
- Jan 2024: 6‑day lapse in BCBS KC when we couldn’t cover $612 premium.
- June 2, 2024: 3 a.m. ambulance from Willow Bend (Overland Park Fire Station 4) to Children’s Mercy ER, 7 hours, $1,200 bill.

4. Questions
- For neuro: realistic plan to add EPIDIOLEX for Daniel AND eventually simplify my own regimen so I can safely drive (no more 135th/Metcalf red‑light incidents) and keep working.
- For PCP: depression management if we change valproate; document impact of sleep loss + seizures on functioning.

Hi Mrs. Alvarez,

Thank you for reaching out about Daniel “refusing” to follow directions in math.

I wanted to share what I saw at home on the evening of September 18, 2024, when we worked on the same Blue Valley Middle math packet at our kitchen table (7–8 p.m.). In that 45‑minute period, he had at least 10 brief staring spells—eyes glazed, slight eyelid flutter, no response to his name. These are his atypical absence seizures. After each one he was confused and had no memory of the instruction I’d just given. So when it looks like he’s ignoring you, it’s very likely he’s actually having a seizure and then missing the step.

He also continues to have dangerous drop seizures at school. On October 3, 2024, around 12:30 p.m., he had a big atonic drop outside Mr. Jenkins’ science room when he’d forgotten his helmet. The nurse called me out of a treatment session at Children’s Mercy Pediatric Therapy Clinic – Overland Park so I could sign the incident report and talk with the assistant principal about possibly moving him back into the special education classroom full‑time. I’m trying hard to avoid that unless it’s truly necessary.

At home, the nights are rough. On August 27, 2024, I watched on the baby monitor as he had a cluster of six tonic seizures between 1:00 and 3:00 a.m., then I slept on the floor in his and Sophie’s room so she wouldn’t wake up terrified again. By morning he was exhausted and irritable, which obviously carries into his school day.

All of this is why I push so hard for accommodations in his IEP—he’s not being willful, his brain is literally cutting in and out. I really appreciate your patience with him and am happy to meet with you and Ms. Reynolds to brainstorm strategies (extra written directions, check‑ins after transitions, etc.).

Best,
Emily Carter

Event: Neurology follow‑up for Daniel – EPIDIOLEX discussion
Date/Time: Monday, 2024-10-28, 2:30–3:30 p.m.
Location: Children’s Mercy Hospital – Neurology Clinic, Kansas City (main campus)
Attendees: Emily, Mark, Dr. Patel

Notes/agenda (for us):

1. Treatment goals
- Review main goals: reduce drop attacks (e.g., 10/9/24 playground fall at Blue Valley Middle → 5 stitches + CT, 6 hours in Children’s Mercy ER), fewer nighttime tonic clusters (avoid another 3 a.m. ambulance like 6/2/24 from Willow Bend via Overland Park Fire Station 4, $1,200 bill), improve daytime alertness so he can stay in small‑group reading instead of dropping like at March 2024 IEP with Ms. Reynolds and Dr. Harper.

2. EPIDIOLEX specifics
- Ask for clear titration schedule in writing to share with school nurse (Prairie Ridge/Blue Valley Middle) and to keep with his seizure action plan.
- Side effects: sleepiness, appetite, behavior/mood. How to distinguish med side effects from his usual post‑ictal irritability (e.g., Thanksgiving 2023 meltdown at my parents’ in Lawrence where he hit Dad and then slept 14 hours).
- Interactions with current meds: levetiracetam 750 BID, clobazam 10/20, valproic acid 250 TID. Long‑term hope to taper valproate and/or clobazam if seizures improve.

3. School/safety
- How to communicate med change to school after the 10/3/24 hallway drop outside Mr. Jenkins’ room and the truancy/social‑worker meeting about multiple early pickups (Jan–May 2024).
- Criteria for reconsidering 1:1 aide / recess restrictions if drops decrease.

4. Emergency plan
- Clarify diazepam nasal spray use after 2/2/24 Overland Park Regional visit where ER doc wrote “parental anxiety.” Want a written rescue plan so we’re not second‑guessing ourselves during titration.

Remember: bring seizure log, insurance cards, list of all meds (including my sertraline 50 mg).

Hi Mark,

Quick recap of today before I crash, so we’re on the same page.

Daniel had another rough day. Because I was stuck at Children’s Mercy documenting that crisis eval this morning, his meds ended up almost an hour late (I didn’t get home until about 8:15). The school nurse at Blue Valley Middle called around 11:30 to say he’d already had four hard atonic drops before noon, including one on the playground that earned him yet another concussion note in his file. It felt like October 21, 2024 all over again.

This is on top of the October 3 hallway incident outside Mr. Jenkins’ science room when he forgot his helmet and hit his head on the concrete. Between that, the 10/9 playground fall that sent us to Children’s Mercy ER for six hours and five stitches, and the May 9, 2024 drop at Oak Ridge that led to the meeting with Ms. Alvarez and Mrs. Jensen about possibly moving him to the Blue Valley Special Education Center, I feel like we’re constantly defending why he should stay in his current placement.

I keep thinking about how our own stuff weaves into this. Your insurance through Blue Valley Southwest is what we depend on, but that $612 BCBS premium that lapsed for six days in January 2024 after the $1,847 Children’s Mercy EEG bill went to collections still hangs over us. And my seizure meds—Keppra 750 BID, Depakote 250 TID, Onfi 10/20—aren’t exactly cheap either, especially since December when we had to pay $186 at CVS on 151st and borrow from Mom’s retirement.

I know we’re both exhausted from the 1–3 a.m. hallway shifts after nights like August 27 (six tonic seizures on the monitor), and from church episodes like November 9 at Colonial Presbyterian when he had that big generalized tonic‑clonic in the aisle. But I also know we’re still a good team.

Let’s go over questions for Dr. Patel about EPIDIOLEX tomorrow night after the kids are down.

Love,
E

Private journal – future planning fears

Daniel’s well visit at College Park Family Care with Dr. Nguyen in August 2024 is still rattling around in my head. She gently raised guardianship vs supported decision‑making for when he turns 18, given the severity of his Lennox‑Gastaut and cognitive delays. Mark and I have always daydreamed about him maybe living in a supported apartment somewhere in Overland Park, with staff checking in, but still some independence. Now it feels like that depends on whether we can get at least a 50–60% seizure reduction over the next 2–3 years.

EPIDIOLEX has become this symbol in my mind. I know it’s not a magic fix, but if it doesn’t move the needle on his drop seizures and nighttime tonic clusters, we’re probably looking at full guardianship and maybe even the kind of residential care Dr. Patel at Children’s Mercy mentioned after his PICU admission on March 3, 2023. That’s why the ER bag sits by the front door and the folder with advance directives/guardianship notes lives in the kitchen drawer.

Meanwhile, the financial side is brutal. Since his KU Med hospitalization last September 2023 and the $1,200 ambulance ride from Overland Park, we’ve been stacking charges on the CareCredit card and even paused Sophie’s 529 contributions in October 2023. Then the $1,847 Children’s Mercy EEG bill went to collections and the BCBS premium lapsed for six days in January 2024. Starting an expensive new med terrifies me.

Layered on top: my own epilepsy (Keppra 750 BID, Onfi 10/20, Depakote 250 TID, Valtoco in my bag after that generalized tonic‑clonic during Daniel’s IEP at Prairie Ridge in Feb 2024), the October 2023 status episode at work, the March 2024 panic attack at 135th & Metcalf, and the sertraline/therapy marathon just to function.

I need to remember: we’re doing the best we can with impossible variables.

metadata.title: Guardianship, EPIDIOLEX, money, and my own brain
